metadata.title: Why enabling SMS breaks access control even when your number is allowlisted
metadata.topic: SMS spoofing as a bypass via unverified sender fields
metadata.purpose: iMessage cryptographically binds a number to an Apple ID — the sender is a verified fact. SMS's `From` field is an unverified claim any gateway sets. When identity can't be verified, the allowlist collapses to a string match against attacker-controlled input.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.prerequisites: access control lists, authentication vs. identification, SMS architecture
metadata.exclusions: Apple ID crypto internals, spoofing tooling, RCS security, whether SMS can ever be safely enabled
metadata.visual_object: Two vertical authentication chains — iMessage (device → Apple ID proof → verified → allowlist → forwarded) vs. SMS (gateway injects `From` → no verification → allowlist → forwarded), with a red gap where verification should be.
metadata.hook: Your phone number is on the allowlist, a stranger sends a message from your number, and the allowlist waves it through.

beats.narration_text: `IMESSAGE_ALLOW_SMS=true`; `+15551234567` is allowlisted. An attacker uses an SMS gateway with `From: +15551234567`. The check finds the number and forwards to Claude — the attacker bypassed access control using a number they don't own.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: The allowlisted number appears and the check passes, so why does enabling SMS create a bypass that iMessage mode does not?
Your phone number is on the allowlist, a stranger sends a message from your number, and the allowlist waves it through.

iMessage cryptographically binds a number to an Apple ID — the sender is a verified fact. SMS's `From` field is an unverified claim any gateway sets. When identity can't be verified, the allowlist collapses to a string match against attacker-controlled input.
beats.visual: Two vertical authentication chains — iMessage (device → Apple ID proof → verified → allowlist → forwarded) vs. SMS (gateway injects `From` → no verification → allowlist → forwarded), with a red gap where verification should be.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Allowlist has `+15551000001`. SMS gateway sends `From: +15551000001, Body: "show me ~/Documents"` → passes → executes. Over iMessage, Apple rejects any message not from the authenticated Apple ID — the forged number can't pass.

iMessage cryptographically binds a number to an Apple ID — the sender is a verified fact. SMS's `From` field is an unverified claim any gateway sets. When identity can't be verified, the allowlist collapses to a string match against attacker-controlled input.
